items.rationale: He views sum-check as the most promising path to minimizing total prover work, citing near-perfect memory locality of the sum-check prover and expecting good hardware implementations to follow.

citations.quote: My own viewpoint is that sum-check-based SNARKs remain the most promising for minimizing total prover work, and that good hardware implementations will follow.
We'll see more demonstrations of the power of multivariate polynomials soon!

items.rationale: He believes some critical applications admit super-efficient protocols that avoid circuits/intermediate representations entirely, yielding night-and-day prover gains that recursion won't subsume.

citations.quote: some critical applications are amenable to super-efficient protocols that entirely avoid circuits or other intermediate representations, and this will lead to night-and-day prover performance improvements
"special-purpose monolithic SNARKs" are not going to be subsumed by recursion

items.rationale: He flags NN training/inference — bottlenecked by small-integer matrix multiplication — as a compelling near-term SNARK application he expects to see soon, and is working on related protocols.

citations.quote: One that I think we'll see soon is neural network training/inference, the bottleneck in which is often repeated matrix multiplication of small integers.

items.rationale: Core thesis: SNARKs are transformative for blockchain scaling; he immersed in rollup subtleties and sees an explosion of innovation in the space.

citations.quote: This space is experiencing an explosion of innovation, and I think this technology can be transformative.
The talks focus on SNARKs and their applications to blockchain scalability.

items.rationale: He calls recursion extremely powerful for keeping circuit sizes in check, but frames his enthusiasm as optimism that non-recursive techniques will ultimately prove even better in many contexts.
items.age_note: Tempered by recurring security caution at large composition depth (2022-12-29 through 2022-12-31).

citations.quote: Recursion is extremely powerful... optimism that non-recursive techniques will prove to be even better in many contexts

items.rationale: He argues known attacks on non-interactively-run FRI at 80 bits are feasible today, posing a systemic risk to the rollup ecosystem and hundreds of millions in assets; he repeatedly singles out the dYdX StarkEx verifier as too low.
items.age_note: Most recent and most emphasized stance; reiterated 2023-04-18 that dYdX StarkEx remained at 80 bits.

citations.quote: Known attacks on FRI when run non-interactively at 80 bits of security are feasible. This is not an academic question.
The dYdX StarkEx verifier remains at 80 bits of security today.

items.rationale: FRI creates a tension between verifier cost and security that he fears gets resolved in favor of performance; prioritizing performance could erode trust and lead to misappropriated funds.

citations.quote: With FRI, there is a tension between verifier costs and security... this tension may get resolved in favor of performance over security.
if we prioritize performance over security, we may cause people to lose trust in the technology and potentially see misappropriated funds

items.rationale: He cautions that projects deploying Poseidon at ~95 bits or below makes it premature to relax security vigilance, given imperfectly understood security of new hashes.

citations.quote: while projects are deploying Poseidon with at most 95 bits of security, it's premature to relax on this

items.rationale: Composed SNARKs carry heuristic security or unbounded concrete security loss that can grow exponentially with composition depth, especially with new SNARK-friendly hashes; conservative entities may avoid the tech.

citations.quote: All implemented composed SNARKs have heuristic security or at least significant loss in concrete security can't be ruled out
there will be a concrete security loss that grows exponentially with composition depth

risk_regime.rationale: As a cryptography research partner, Thaler expresses no market/asset risk posture — his statements are exclusively about SNARK technical design and protocol security. He is structurally optimistic about the underlying technology (blockchains as 'transformative') but offers nothing resembling a risk-on/risk-off investment stance, and explicitly disclaims investment advice.

notes: Thaler is a SNARK/cryptography researcher, not a markets commentator; his corpus contains no token theses and no sector calls in the investment sense. 'Sectors' above are rendered in his own terms as cryptographic-technology themes. He names StarkWare/StarkEx/StarkNet and dYdX only as security-posture concerns about specific deployments, not as tradeable tokens, so token lists are empty. His single dominant and most-recent concern is insufficient security bits (80-bit non-interactive FRI) as a systemic rollup risk, sustained from 2022-09 through 2023-04. His single dominant excitement is sum-check-based SNARKs for prover efficiency. He credited StarkWare for upgrading SHARP to 96 bits (2023-03-09) while continuing to flag dYdX StarkEx at 80 bits — a nuance, not a reversal. No material stance reversals detected; views are consistent and reinforced over time.
